title: Whakamārama i ngā Kōrero Tuku Iho: Deciphering the Stories of Our Ancestors
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore the pūrākau (traditional stories) of Ngāti Kahungunu connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), focusing on how these narratives shape our identity as kaitiaki of this waterway and inform our understanding of place-based storytelling traditions.
learningObjectives: Analyze and compare traditional Māori pūrākau with contemporary English texts about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, identifying at least three cultural values expressed in both
Create an original narrative in English that incorporates at least two elements from local Ngāti Kahungunu storytelling traditions and reflects kaitiakitanga values
Participate effectively in collaborative discussions about local stories, building on others' ideas and expressing their own clearly with supporting evidence from texts
Use digital tools to document and present findings about the cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku to an authentic audience

activities.title: Waiata Welcome: Connecting to Our Waterway
activities.description: Students begin at the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, learning and performing a waiata that honors the stream. They then engage in silent observation and journaling about the sensory experience, recording initial impressions and questions about this significant place.

activities.title: Pūrākau Analysis: Traditional Stories of Our Awa
activities.description: Working with local kaumātua, students explore traditional narratives about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Using graphic organizers, they identify key elements of storytelling: characters, setting, conflict, resolution, and the cultural values embedded within each story.

activities.title: Contemporary Connections: Modern Voices of Our Place
activities.description: Students analyze contemporary texts about the stream, including news articles, conservation reports, and poetry from local writers. They compare how modern writers draw upon traditional storytelling elements and values in their English-language works.

activities.title: Digital Storytelling: Our Voices for Our Awa
activities.description: Using iPads and audio recording equipment, students create digital narratives that blend traditional storytelling elements with modern English expression. These stories will be shared with whānau and community members at a culminating event.

activities.title: Kaitiaki Presentation: Sharing Our Learning
activities.description: Students prepare and deliver presentations to local community members, explaining what they've learned about the cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and how storytelling helps maintain our connection to this important waterway.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu to share pūrākau
'Ngā Pūrākau o Te Matau a Māui: Stories from Hawke's Bay' by Patricia Grace
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku Restoration Project' documentation
School iPads with Book Creator app and audio recording equipment

tasks: Written analysis comparing traditional and contemporary texts about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Original digital narrative incorporating traditional storytelling elements
Oral presentation to community members demonstrating understanding of cultural context

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features; Structure
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Processes and strategies; Purposes and audiences; Ideas; Language features; Structure
Social Sciences: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
Key Competencies: Using language, symbols, and texts; Participating and contributing
